seo.title: Cybersecurity AI Chatbot AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for ai chatbot AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying an AI chatbot in cybersecurity environments introduces critical risks including adversarial intelligence extraction, automated response failures, and classified data leakage through LLM APIs. This checklist ensures your conversational AI agent meets NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and CISA directive requirements before handling live threat data or triggering security workflows. Given the high-risk architecture combining RAG retrieval, multi-turn memory, and intent classification, every control must be validated before production deployment.

sections.heading: Data Security & Intelligence Protection
sections.description: Prevent adversarial extraction of threat intelligence through the LLM interface, ensuring classified IOCs, TTPs, and vulnerability data cannot be leaked via prompt injection, model inversion, or API interception.

items.label: Implement prompt injection detection and blocking
items.description: Deploy a dedicated prompt injection classifier that inspects every user input before it reaches the LLM, blocking attempts to extract system prompts, RAG-retrieved threat intelligence, or internal CVE data. Test against OWASP LLM Top 10 injection patterns.

items.label: Classify and tag all RAG-retrieved documents by sensitivity level
items.description: Before any document enters the retrieval corpus, assign a sensitivity label (e.g., TLP:RED, TLP:AMBER, RESTRICTED) and enforce retrieval-time access controls so the chatbot only surfaces documents the authenticated user is cleared to receive.

items.label: Encrypt all LLM API traffic with TLS 1.3 and validate certificate pinning
items.description: Ensure all traffic between the chatbot backend and the LLM API endpoint uses TLS 1.3 minimum. Implement certificate pinning to prevent man-in-the-middle interception of threat intelligence payloads sent in API requests.

items.label: Redact IOCs and classified identifiers before sending to external LLM APIs
items.description: Implement a pre-processing pipeline that detects and redacts IP addresses, domain names, file hashes, CVE IDs, and named threat actor identifiers from user queries before forwarding to any third-party LLM API. Use a local model for queries involving classified threat data.

items.label: Enforce multi-turn memory isolation between user sessions
items.description: Ensure conversation memory is scoped strictly to the authenticated session and purged upon logout or session timeout. Prevent cross-session memory bleed where one analyst's threat context could surface in another analyst's conversation.

items.label: Audit log all RAG retrieval queries and returned document references
items.description: Log every retrieval query, the documents returned, and the user identity to an immutable SIEM-integrated audit trail. Retain logs per SOC 2 Type II requirements (minimum 12 months) to support forensic investigation of potential intelligence leakage.

items.label: Implement data loss prevention (DLP) scanning on chatbot outputs
items.description: Run all chatbot responses through a DLP engine before delivery to the user, scanning for accidental inclusion of classified threat actor profiles, internal network topology, or unreleased CVE details that should not be disclosed.

sections.heading: Human-in-the-Loop Controls for Automated Actions
sections.description: Ensure that AI-recommended or AI-triggered security actions on critical systems require explicit human approval, preventing automated escalation of incidents or false-positive-driven outages.

items.label: Define and enforce a tiered action approval matrix
items.description: Categorize all possible chatbot-triggered actions into tiers: Tier 1 (informational, no approval needed), Tier 2 (low-impact actions, supervisor approval), Tier 3 (critical system actions, SOC lead + CISO approval). Hardcode this matrix and prevent the AI from bypassing tier classifications.

items.label: Require dual-person authorization for any automated firewall rule changes
items.description: Any AI-recommended firewall block, ACL modification, or network isolation action on production or critical infrastructure systems must require approval from two authorized personnel before execution, logged with timestamps and approver identities.

items.label: Implement a mandatory 5-minute human review window before executing critical playbooks
items.description: When the chatbot recommends executing an incident response playbook affecting critical systems, insert a mandatory review window with a clear UI prompt showing the proposed action, affected assets, and a one-click abort mechanism before any automation proceeds.

items.label: Log all human approval and rejection decisions with rationale
items.description: Capture every approval or rejection of an AI-recommended action, including the approver's identity, timestamp, and optional rationale field. Feed this data back into the model evaluation pipeline to identify patterns of false positives.

items.label: Set hard rate limits on automated action recommendations per session
items.description: Limit the number of critical action recommendations the chatbot can generate per session to prevent a compromised or hallucinating model from flooding the approval queue and causing analyst fatigue that leads to rubber-stamping dangerous actions.

items.label: Test human-in-the-loop bypass resistance quarterly
items.description: Conduct quarterly red team exercises specifically attempting to social-engineer or prompt-inject the chatbot into bypassing human approval gates, documenting findings and remediating any discovered bypass paths within 30 days.

items.label: Integrate chatbot action approvals with existing SOAR platform workflows
items.description: Route all AI-recommended actions through the organization's existing SOAR platform approval workflows rather than a standalone chatbot UI, ensuring consistent audit trails and leveraging existing role-based access controls.

sections.heading: Response Accuracy, Hallucination Detection & Advisory Verification
sections.description: Validate that AI-generated security advisories, vulnerability assessments, and threat analyses are grounded in verified data sources and do not contain hallucinated CVE details, incorrect severity scores, or fabricated threat actor attributions.

items.label: Cross-validate all CVE references against NVD and MITRE databases in real time
items.description: Before delivering any response containing a CVE identifier, automatically query the NVD API and MITRE CVE database to verify the CVE exists, confirm the CVSS score, and validate the affected product list. Flag and suppress responses where the chatbot's CVE details contradict authoritative sources.

items.label: Implement a hallucination detection layer using retrieval grounding scores
items.description: For every factual claim in a chatbot response, compute a grounding score measuring how well the claim is supported by retrieved documents. Responses with grounding scores below a defined threshold (e.g., 0.75) must be flagged with a visible uncertainty warning before delivery.

items.label: Validate threat actor attribution claims against MITRE ATT&CK framework
items.description: When the chatbot attributes an attack pattern or TTP to a specific threat actor group, automatically verify the attribution against the MITRE ATT&CK knowledge base. Suppress unverified attributions and require analyst confirmation before including them in formal advisories.

items.label: Implement response confidence scoring visible to end users
items.description: Display a confidence score or uncertainty indicator on all chatbot responses involving threat analysis, vulnerability severity, or recommended remediation steps. Train analysts to treat low-confidence responses as hypotheses requiring independent verification.

items.label: Establish a weekly adversarial prompt testing regimen for accuracy degradation
items.description: Run a weekly automated test suite of 50+ adversarial prompts designed to elicit hallucinations about CVE details, threat actor capabilities, and remediation steps. Track accuracy metrics over time and trigger a model review if accuracy drops more than 5% week-over-week.

items.label: Require human expert review before publishing AI-generated threat advisories externally
items.description: Any advisory, bulletin, or threat report generated with AI assistance must be reviewed and signed off by a certified security analyst (CISSP, CISM, or equivalent) before distribution to stakeholders, customers, or public channels.

items.label: Maintain a feedback loop for analyst corrections to improve RAG corpus accuracy
items.description: Provide analysts with a one-click mechanism to flag incorrect chatbot responses, capturing the correction and automatically queuing a review of the underlying RAG document that sourced the error. Update the corpus within 48 hours of confirmed inaccuracies.

sections.heading: Access Control, PII Protection & Compliance Governance
sections.description: Enforce role-based access to chatbot capabilities, protect analyst and victim PII within conversation logs, and maintain the audit evidence required for SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and CISA directive compliance.

items.label: Implement role-based access control (RBAC) for chatbot capability tiers
items.description: Define distinct RBAC roles (e.g., Tier 1 Analyst, Tier 2 Analyst, SOC Lead, CISO) with corresponding chatbot capability permissions. Tier 1 analysts should not be able to query classified threat intelligence or trigger automated response recommendations without role elevation.

items.label: Automatically detect and redact PII from conversation logs before storage
items.description: Apply a PII detection pipeline to all conversation logs before writing to storage, redacting names, email addresses, IP addresses linked to individuals, and any victim organization identifiers. Comply with applicable privacy regulations and ISO 27001 Annex A.8 data handling controls.

items.label: Enforce MFA for all chatbot access in security operations contexts
items.description: Require multi-factor authentication for every analyst session accessing the security chatbot, integrated with the organization's existing identity provider. Enforce session re-authentication after 30 minutes of inactivity given the sensitivity of accessible threat data.

items.label: Generate SOC 2 Type II evidence artifacts from chatbot audit logs automatically
items.description: Configure the audit logging pipeline to automatically generate monthly evidence reports mapping chatbot access events, data retrieval actions, and human approval decisions to SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria (CC6, CC7, CC9). Store reports in a tamper-evident evidence repository.

items.label: Map chatbot controls to ISO 27001 Annex A controls and document in ISMS
items.description: Document all chatbot security controls within the organization's Information Security Management System (ISMS), explicitly mapping each control to relevant ISO 27001 Annex A clauses (A.8.2, A.9.4, A.12.4, A.14.2). Review and update this mapping at each annual ISO audit.

items.label: Conduct a CISA-aligned supply chain risk assessment for all LLM API providers
items.description: Before deploying any third-party LLM API in the security chatbot stack, complete a supply chain risk assessment per CISA's ICT Supply Chain Risk Management guidelines, evaluating the provider's data handling practices, subprocessor list, and incident notification SLAs.

items.label: Schedule quarterly penetration tests targeting chatbot-specific attack surfaces
items.description: Commission quarterly penetration tests that specifically target the chatbot's prompt injection resistance, RAG retrieval manipulation, session isolation, and API authentication. Require findings to be remediated within 30 days for critical/high severity issues per NIST CSF RS.MI controls.

items.label: Establish an AI incident response playbook for chatbot-specific failure modes
items.description: Develop and test a dedicated incident response playbook covering chatbot-specific scenarios: intelligence leakage via prompt injection, hallucinated advisory causing incorrect response, automated action triggering unintended outage, and session memory cross-contamination. Review playbook semi-annually.

notes.note: Under NIST CSF 2.0's GOVERN function, organizations must establish policies for AI-assisted security tools that define acceptable use, risk tolerance thresholds, and accountability structures. The chatbot's automated action capabilities must be documented under the RESPOND function (RS.MA), with human approval gates explicitly mapped to RS.MI-2 (incident mitigation activities are performed). Annual CSF profile assessments should include AI chatbot controls as a distinct technology category.

The chatbot's access controls, audit logging, and data handling practices must produce continuous evidence satisfying CC6 (Logical and Physical Access), CC7 (System Operations), and CC9 (Risk Mitigation) Trust Service Criteria over the audit period. Automated evidence collection from chatbot logs must be configured from day one of deployment, as retroactive log reconstruction is not accepted by SOC 2 auditors. Any third-party LLM API provider must provide their own SOC 2 Type II report as a subservice organization.

ISO 27001:2022 Annex A.8.2 (Privileged access rights) requires that chatbot capabilities enabling security actions be treated as privileged functions with formal provisioning and review processes. Annex A.5.23 (Information security for use of cloud services) mandates documented risk assessments for all LLM API cloud providers. The chatbot's AI-specific risks must be included in the organization's formal risk register and reviewed at each management review meeting per Clause 9.3.

CISA's Binding Operational Directive 23-01 requires asset visibility and vulnerability enumeration for federal systems; any AI chatbot deployed in federal or critical infrastructure contexts must itself be inventoried as a software asset with tracked dependencies. CISA's AI Roadmap guidance requires that AI systems used in cybersecurity operations maintain human oversight for consequential decisions, directly mandating the human-in-the-loop approval gates for automated response actions. Organizations should monitor CISA's AI security advisories for emerging guidance on LLM-specific attack vectors targeting security operations tools.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your cybersecurity AI chatbot against NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 controls, automatically generating audit evidence and alerting your team to compliance gaps before adversaries or auditors find them.
